skill.name: parallel-task-spark
skill.description: Coordinates parallel AI subagents to execute markdown development plans with task dependencies, validation, status logs, and commits.
skill.summary: Run dependency-aware development plans through parallel subagent execution.
skill.author: am-will

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged markdown backticks, fenced examples, and ordinary prompt text as shell execution and weak cryptography. Those static findings are false positives because SKILL.md contains no executable script, network call, or cryptographic implementation. The remaining risk is workflow-based: the skill delegates plan content to subagents that edit files and create commits, so users should review trusted plan files before running it.

medium_findings.title: Plan Content Delegated To File-Changing Subagents
medium_findings.description: The skill parses a user-provided plan and passes task content into subagent prompts that instruct agents to inspect files, implement changes, validate work, update plan logs, and commit. This is legitimate for a development orchestration skill, but a malicious or unreviewed plan file could steer broad workspace changes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line evidence directly shows plan parsing and delegation to subagents that perform file edits and commits. The behavior appears intended rather than malicious, so the risk is medium instead of high.

medium_findings.title: Automated Commit Workflow Can Persist Unwanted Changes
medium_findings.description: The subagent instructions require each worker to commit its changes and later require each wave of work to be committed. This can make unintended changes more durable if a task is incorrect or if the plan file contains unsafe instructions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commit requirement is explicit and tied to file-modifying development tasks. It does not include pushing changes or credential access, which limits severity.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Execution Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution at markdown inline code spans, fenced prompt templates, and example usage blocks. SKILL.md contains documentation and prompt instructions, not Ruby code or shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced locations are markdown formatting and examples. No executable backtick operator, command runner, or script file is present.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Findings Are Text Matches Only
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts map to frontmatter, template labels, and ordinary prompt text such as task descriptions and stopping behavior. No hash function, cipher, key handling, or cryptographic API appears in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reported lines do not implement or reference cryptographic algorithms. The finding appears caused by substring or keyword matching in non-code text.

low_findings.title: Static System Reconnaissance Finding Is A Role Constraint
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alert points to an instruction requiring agent_type to be sparky. This is role selection text for subagent orchestration, not host enumeration or system discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line contains a fixed role constraint and no system command, filesystem scan, network probe, or environment inspection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Untrusted Plan Instructions Enter Worker Prompts
dangerous_patterns.description: Plan task descriptions and constraints are inserted into subagent task prompts. Users should treat plan files as instructions with authority to guide file edits and validation work.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The template clearly forwards plan content into worker prompts. No malicious payload exists in the skill itself, but the design depends on the safety of the supplied plan.

content.value_statement: Large development plans are slow when every task waits on one agent. This skill parses plan dependencies and coordinates parallel subagents to complete, validate, log, and commit work.
actual_capabilities: Parses markdown plan files for task sections and dependency lists.
Filters execution to selected task IDs and required dependencies.
Launches unblocked tasks in parallel using the sparky subagent role.
Provides a reusable task prompt template for implementation work.
Checks task completion, validation results, plan logs, and commit status.
Repeats task waves until all dependency-ready work is complete.
limitations: Requires a structured markdown plan with recognizable task headings.
Does not include a parser script or automated dependency resolver.
Depends on available subagent orchestration support in the host tool.
Can amplify unsafe instructions if the plan file is untrusted.

use_cases.title: Execute multi-part implementation plans
use_cases.description: Break a prepared coding plan into dependency-aware waves and assign ready tasks to parallel workers.

use_cases.title: Coordinate refactor work across files
use_cases.description: Use task dependencies to keep shared changes ordered while independent file updates proceed at the same time.

use_cases.title: Track agent completion and validation
use_cases.description: Require each worker to update plan logs, report validation, and summarize modified files before the next wave starts.

prompt_templates.title: Run selected tasks
prompt_templates.scenario: Execute only a focused set of tasks and their dependencies.

prompt_templates.title: Resume a blocked plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Continue work after reviewing a partially completed plan.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate a dependency-heavy release plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced use for staged implementation, validation, and commit checks.

output_examples.output: The skill identifies independent setup tasks, starts them together, waits for validation summaries, then starts dependent integration tasks.

output_examples.input: Run only the profile UI tasks.
output_examples.output: The skill includes required dependencies, skips unrelated tasks, and reports which files were changed and which checks were deferred.

output_examples.input: Continue after a failed validation step.
output_examples.output: The skill asks the responsible worker to retry or escalate, then records the issue before launching the next available task wave.

best_practices: Review plan files before running them, especially when they come from outside your team.
Keep task sections small, atomic, and linked to clear validation steps.
Inspect commits from each wave before allowing later dependent tasks to proceed.
anti_patterns: Do not use vague plan tasks that leave file scope or acceptance criteria undefined.
Do not run untrusted plans that can instruct agents to edit sensitive files.
Do not treat a committed worker result as reviewed or production ready.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It coordinates parallel subagents that execute tasks from a markdown development plan.

faq.question: Does it create code by itself?
faq.answer: It delegates implementation to subagents using task details from the plan file.

faq.question: What plan format works best?
faq.answer: Use markdown task headings, explicit depends_on lists, acceptance criteria, and validation steps.

faq.question: Can I run only part of a plan?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill supports selected task IDs and includes required dependencies.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill intentionally edits files and commits work through subagents, so unsafe plan files can have broad effects.

faq.question: Does the skill push changes to a remote repository?
faq.answer: No. The instructions explicitly say to commit only and never push.
